title: Lacmellea utilis
intro: Lacmellea utilis, also known as the "monkey ladder," is a perennial plant species in the Apocynaceae family native to tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues, due to its purported health benefits of anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. Lacmellea utilis contains bioactive constituents such as alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, including lacmelleine, lacmelleic acid, and ursolic acid, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly prepared into herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, and is also used in traditional remedies, such as ointments and salves, to treat skin conditions and wounds.
taxonomy: Lacmellea utilis is classified in the family Apocynaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. This family includes over 5,000 species of plants, many of which are native to tropical and subtropical regions. The genus Lacmellea is a part of this family and comprises several species of vines and shrubs. Lacmellea utilis, in particular, is a species of plant that is native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. Its classification reflects its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the Apocynaceae family.

It is said to possess antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds, which contribute to its potential health benefits.
